note_data.title: Experimental design and separation techniques
keyPoints: **Pure substances** have fixed melting and boiling points, while impure substances melt/boil over a range of temperatures
**Simple distillation** separates liquids with very different boiling points (difference >25°C) or separates a solvent from dissolved solids
**Fractional distillation** separates liquids with similar boiling points using a fractionating column packed with glass beads
**Paper chromatography** separates mixtures based on different solubilities in the solvent - more soluble substances travel further
**Rf value** = distance travelled by substance ÷ distance travelled by solvent front (always less than 1)
**Crystallisation** purifies soluble solids by dissolving in minimum hot water, filtering hot, then cooling to form crystals
**Filtration** separates insoluble solids from liquids using filter paper in a funnel
In experimental design, **control variables** must be kept constant to ensure a fair test
**Locating agents** like iodine vapour or ninhydrin are used to make colourless spots visible in chromatography

definitions.definition: A substance that contains only one type of element or compound with fixed melting and boiling points
A separation technique that separates mixtures based on the different solubilities of substances in a solvent
The ratio of the distance travelled by a substance to the distance travelled by the solvent front
A separation technique that separates liquids based on their different boiling points
A purification technique where a solute forms crystals when its hot saturated solution is cooled

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Give the point + reason using 'because' or 'this is because'
Give step-by-step method in logical order with apparatus mentioned
Give a reasonable scientific idea based on the information provided

modelAnswer.question: A student wants to separate and purify salt from a mixture of salt and sand. Describe the method the student should use.
modelAnswer.answer: Add distilled water to dissolve the salt. Filter the mixture to separate the sand (residue) from the salt solution (filtrate). Heat the filtrate gently to evaporate the water and obtain pure salt crystals.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Add water to dissolve salt
Mark 4: Evaporate water to obtain salt crystals

commonMistakes: Never say 'boil the solution' for crystallisation - always say 'heat gently' or 'evaporate'
Students often confuse simple and fractional distillation - remember the 25°C boiling point difference rule
In chromatography, never say spots 'dissolve' in solvent - say they are 'carried up' or 'travel with' the solvent
Don't forget to mention 'minimum amount of hot water' in crystallisation methods
Students mix up residue and filtrate - residue stays on filter paper, filtrate passes through
examinerTips: Separation techniques appear in every paper - learn the apparatus diagrams
Always calculate Rf values to 2 decimal places and show your working
For purity tests, mention both melting point AND boiling point determination
In method questions, use command words like 'heat', 'filter', 'collect' for clear instructions
formulas: Rf value = distance travelled by substance ÷ distance travelled by solvent front
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Simple distillation setup showing flask with mixture, thermometer, condenser with cold water in/out, and collection beaker. Fractional distillation has additional fractionating column with glass beads between flask and condenser.
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q4 (6 marks) — they asked students to design an experiment to investigate the effect of temperature on reaction rate, requiring identification of variables and control methods
N/D 2022 P1 Q12 (2 marks) — tested separation of salt and sand mixture, many students forgot to mention evaporation of water from filtrate
M/J 2022 P2 Q6 (8 marks) — required students to separate a mixture of ethanol, water and copper sulfate crystals using multiple techniques in correct sequence
N/D 2023 P2 Q3 (5 marks) — asked for experimental design to compare reactivity of metals, focusing on fair testing and safety considerations

workedExample.question: Design an experiment to investigate whether particle size affects the rate of reaction between marble chips and hydrochloric acid. Your answer should include the method, variables to control, and safety precautions. (8 marks)
steps: Step 1: Identify independent variable (particle size of marble chips) and dependent variable (rate of reaction measured by volume of CO2 produced)
Step 2: List controlled variables - concentration of HCl, volume of HCl, mass of marble chips, temperature
Step 3: Describe method - use measuring cylinder to collect CO2 gas, record volume every 30 seconds for 5 minutes
Step 4: Specify safety precautions - wear safety goggles, use fume cupboard, handle acid carefully
Step 5: Explain how to ensure reliability - repeat experiment 3 times and calculate mean
workedExample.answer: Use marble chips of different sizes (large, medium, small) with same mass (5g each). Add each sample to 50cm³ of 1M HCl in conical flask connected to measuring cylinder via delivery tube. Record volume of CO2 collected every 30 seconds. Control temperature, acid concentration and volume. Wear safety goggles and work in fume cupboard. Repeat each test 3 times for reliability.
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark each for: independent/dependent variables, 3 control variables, method for measuring gas, safety precaution, reliability measure

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates failed to distinguish between controlled variables and the variable being measured' — always clearly state what you are keeping constant versus what you are changing

Examiner Report 2022: 'Students often describe separation techniques in wrong order' — for complex mixtures, always consider physical properties first (solubility, boiling point, particle size) before choosing technique sequence
Examiner Report 2023: 'Candidates lose marks by not explaining WHY a technique works' — always link the separation method to the different physical properties being exploited
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw a complete filtration setup showing: conical funnel in neck of conical flask, filter paper folded correctly in funnel, residue remaining on filter paper, clear filtrate collected in flask below. Include labels for all components and show mixture being poured down glass rod to prevent splashing. For distillation, draw complete apparatus with thermometer at correct height, condenser with cold water flow direction marked, and separate collection vessels.
note_data.quickRecap: Experimental design requires clear identification of independent, dependent and controlled variables plus reliability measures. Separation techniques exploit different physical properties - filtration for insoluble solids, distillation for different boiling points, crystallization for different solubilities. Always consider the sequence of techniques needed for complex mixtures. Safety precautions and accurate labelled diagrams are essential for full marks.
